contents: Skin Manifestations Associated with Autoimmune Liver Diseases:a Systematic ReviewBenedetta Terziroli Beretta-Piccoli1& Pietro Invernizzi2 & M. Eric Gershwin3 &Carlo Mainetti4# Springer Science+Business Media, LLC 2017Abstract Autoimmune liver diseases, which include mainlyautoimmune hepatitis, primary biliary cholangitis, primarysclerosing cholangitis, and the variant syndromes, are oftenassociated with extrahepatic autoimmune diseases. However,the association with cutaneous diseases is less well described.In the present article, we provide a systematic literature reviewon skin manifestations linked to each of these four autoim-mune liver diseases, excluding skin manifestations of system-ic diseases. The association of autoimmune hepatitis with vit-iligo is well known, with a particular striking association withtype 2 autoimmune hepatitis, a condition occurring almostentirely in children and adolescents, much rarer and moreaggressive than type 1 autoimmune hepatitis; probable asso-ciations are also identified with alopecia areata, psoriasis,and pyoderma gangrenosum. Primary biliary cholangitisis not linked to lichen planus as previously assumed, butto vitiligo, psoriasis and the very rare amicrobialpustulosis of the folds. The proposed diagnostic criteriafor this latter condition include the presence of anti-mitochondrial autoantibodies, the serological hallmark ofprimary biliary cholangitis. The very strong association ofprimary sclerosing cholangitis with inflammatory bowel

diseases hampers the search for an association with skindiseases, since inflammatory bowel diseases have a strongassociation with various dermatological condition, includ-ing neutrophilic dermatoses and erythema nodosum.Nevertheless, a probable association of primary sclerosingcholangitis with psoriasis is identified in this review.Variant syndromes, also called overlap syndromes, arelikely associated with vitiligo as well, which is not sur-prising, since autoimmune hepatitis is a feature of theseconditions and they may share regions of the MHC.Keywords Autoimmune hepatitis . Primary biliarycholangitis . Primary sclerosing cholangitis . Variantsyndrome . Vitiligo . Alopecia areata . Psoriasis . IntroductionAcute and chronic liver diseases are associated withmany, unspecific dermatological signs and symptoms.Acute liver injury is frequently associated with jaundiceand pruritus. In addition, more specific dermatologicalmanifestations linked to the underlying cause of liverdisease may be present, such as drug-induced hypersen-sitivity reactions characterized by fever, lymphadenopa-thy, eosinophilia, hepatic damage, and cutaneous reac-tions, referred to as drug reaction with eosinophilia andsystemic symptoms (DRESS) (Fig. 1). Other cutaneouschanges linked to the cause of the liver disease includebrown discoloration of the skin in hemochromatosis(bronze diabetes) and bluish discoloration of naillunulae in Wilson's disease (azure lunulae).Liver cirrhosis is the shared final result of any chronic liverinjury, and its dermatological stigmata are well recognized. Arecent series from Russia of 368 patients with chronic liverdiseases of different etiologies reported some kind ofcoexisting skin conditions in 86.4% [1]. Cutaneous changesassociated with liver cirrhosis include jaundice and scleralicterus, pruritus and prurigo nodularis, excoriations, gyneco-mastia, alopecia, xanthelasma, nail lesions, and vascularchanges such as spider telangiectases, palmar erythema, papermoney skin, and dilated abdominal wall veins [2, 3]. Anotherlarge series again from Russia of patients with chronic liverdisease of different origin found 72/660 (11%) of the patientsbeing affected by cutaneous vasculitis, mainly cirrhotic patients,

and the authors propose that this cutaneous disorder may suggesta diagnosis of chronic liver disease [4]. Nail changes associatedwith hypoalbuminemia of liver cirrhosis include Muehrcke nails[5], characterized by transverse white bands, koilonychias,clubbed nails, and Terry nails [6]. The latter are characterizedby a white opaque nail plate with only the distal part retainingits normal pink color. All these cutaneous and nail findings arenot related to the specific cause of liver cirrhosis. Their pres-ence should prompt the clinicians to search for an underlingliver disease.The most frequent and best-characterized autoimmuneliver diseases are autoimmune hepatitis (AIH), primarybiliary cholangitis (PBC), and primary sclerosingcholangitis (PSC). A subgroup of patients presents withclinical, biochemical, serological, and histological fea-tures of both AIH and PBC, or AIH and PSC. Theseconditions are referred to as overlap syndromes, al-though some experts suggest the name Bvariantsyndromes,^ to point out that they should be classifiedaccording to the predominant disease, rather than beconsidered separate entities [7]. The pathologies canpresent either simultaneously or consecutively.Methodology

This article provides a systematic review of reported skin dis-eases associated with PBC, AIH, PSC, and variant syndromes.Immunoglobulin-4-associated cholangitis is notdiscussed in this review. Cutaneous infections, skin cancer,and skin manifestations associated with unspecific liver dis-ease are not included. Concomitant systemic autoimmune dis-eases with skin manifestations such as systemic lupus erythe-matosus, systemic sclerosis, CREST syndrome, Sjogren'ssyndrome and dermatomyositis, or sarcoidosis (Fig. 2) werenot considered in this article as being skin diseases. Inaccuratereports of concomitant diseases, such as Bvasculitis,^ were notincluded in the review.For the purpose of the systematic review of the litera-ture, publications cited in PubMed ( were selected using the search wordsBautoimmune hepatitis^ and Bskin,^ Bprimary biliarycirrhosis/cholangitis^ and Bskin,^ and Bprimary sclerosingcholangitis^ and Bskin.^ Citations were chosen on the basisof their relevance to the aim of this article. Fundamentalcharacteristics of the abstracts judged pertinent to the reviewwere noted, and full-length original articles were selectedfrom the abstracts.Autoimmune HepatitisAIH is a rare chronic inflammation of the liver characterizedby elevated transaminase and immunoglobulin G levels, pos-itive autoantibodies, histological evidence of interface hepati-tis, and a striking response to steroid treatment [8]. If untreat-ed, AIH has a severe prognosis, with a 5-year survival ratebelow 25% [9], but if adequately treated, the 10-year survivalrate is about 90% [10]. Its pathogenesis is unknown: it isbelieved that the disease is initiated by the presentation of aliver autoantigen to a naive CD4 effector T cell by antigenpresenting cells within the liver, in a genetically predisposedsubject [11, 12]. Potential viral triggers include hepatitis Cvirus, hepatitis B virus, hepatitis E virus, cytomegalovirus,

and herpes simplex virus [11]. Non-viral environmental trig-gers include drugs and herbal remedies, the best documentedbeing nitrofurantoin, minocycline, statins, and anti-tumor ne-crosis factor (TNF) agents [8]. It affects all ages and races, andhas, as many autoimmune diseases, a female preponderance,with a reported female-to-male ratio of 4:1 [10]. The preciseincidence and prevalence of AIH is unknown, due to the ret-rospective nature of many studies, many of them performedbefore the publication of standardized diagnostic criteria [13].The reported annual incidence ranges between 1.9 per100,000 inhabitants in Norway [14] and 3 per 100,000 inhab-itants in the UK [15]. The reported prevalence ranges from11.6 per 100,000 inhabitants in Spain [16] to 35.9 per 100,000inhabitants in Alaskan natives [17]. The reported disease fre-quency is increasing in recent years, probably reflecting a trueincrease of disease frequency, and not only a better case as-certainment [8]. Presenting symptoms are unspecific in 40-60% of adult patients, including fatigue, nausea, arthralgia,amenorrhea, and abdominal pain [18]. Twenty to thirty per-cent of patients present with an acute icteric hepatitis [19], andinfrequently, acute liver failure is the first manifestation ofautoimmune hepatitis [20]. According to the serological pro-file, autoimmune hepatitis is divided into type 1, positive foranti-nuclear (ANA) and/or anti-smooth muscle autoantibodies(anti-SMA), and type 2, positive for anti-liver kidney type 1(anti-LKM-1) and/or anti-liver cytosol type 1 (anti-LC-1) au-toantibodies [12]. Type 2 affects mostly children, adolescents,and young adults, accounting for one third of pediatric casesand has a more aggressive course [21]. In pediatrics, AIH isassociated with macroscopic biliary tree changes in up to halfof the patients [22], a condition known as autoimmune scle-rosing cholangitis. Treatment of AIH is based on corticoste-roids, with azathioprine used as a steroid-sparing agent [8].The recommended initial dose of systemic steroids is 1 mg/kgin adults and 2 mg/kg in children (up to 60 mg/day) [8, 9],which has to be rapidly tapered to a maintenance dose of 2.5-7.5 mg/day, depending on age, comorbidities, and diseaseseverity. Azathioprine should be added only after a few weeks,particularly in jaundiced patients, because of its potential hep-atotoxicity [8, 9]. Eighty to ninety percent of patients achievecomplete disease control with this standard treatment, basedon trials performed in the 1970 [23-25]. Second-line treat-ments include mycophenolate mofetil, calcineurine inhibitors,and other immunosuppressive drugs. A minority of patientshas resistant disease and ultimately need liver transplantation.Both types 1 and 2 AIH are associated with extrahepaticautoimmune disorders in about 20-40% of the patients, themost frequent being Hashimoto thyroiditis, Grave's disease,inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), and type I diabetes [8, 19].Females are more often affected by extrahepatic autoimmuneconditions [26-30]. The presence of extrahepatic autoimmunediseases does not affect the prognosis of AIH [30]. The coex-istence of concomitant personal or family autoimmunity

is highly characteristic in AIH, being part of the original andrevised scoring systems prosed by the InternationalAutoimmune Hepatitis Group [13, 31]. One recent retrospec-tive large study from the UK reported an association betweenpositive family history for autoimmunity and presence of ex-trahepatic autoimmune diseases in AIH patients [30]. Theprevalence of concomitant autoimmune skin diseases hasbeen evaluated in different cohorts of AIH patients(Table 1). In addition, sporadic case reports have been pub-lished (Table 1). There are also series of patients with derma-tological diseases reporting concomitant extra-cutaneous dis-eases [32-34].Since the treatment of AIH is based on immunosuppres-sion, skin infections frequently occur in AIH patients but willnot be discussed here. In addition, immunosuppressed patientshave an increased incidence of skin cancer. A recent retrospec-tive study from the USA [35] showed an increased risk of non-melanoma skin cancer in AIH patients on immunosuppressivetreatment, the risk being also independently associated withage and age at diagnosis. A retrospective large cohort fromSweden also reported an increased risk of non-melanoma skincancer in AIH patients, reported in 7/467 cases, 5 of thembeing exposed to azathioprine for years [36].Autoimmune polyendocrinopathy-candidiasis-ectodermaldystrophy is a rare genetic autosomal recessive disorder withvariable phenotype, including liver involvement resemblingtype 2 autoimmune hepatitis in 10-20% of patients and cuta-neous autoimmune and infectious involvement, particularlychronic mucocutaneous candidiasis [37] and also alopeciaareata and vitiligo [38]. Awareness of this rare condition isimportant, particularly in pediatrics [39] [40]. This rare syn-drome is not included in this review.An interesting population-based study from Denmark re-ported a higher incidence of AIH in patients with psoriasis[41]. The authors speculated a common pathogenetic role oftissue resident memory T cells in both diseases: however, thishas been demonstrated for psoriasis but not for AIH. In addi-tion, the methodology to identify AIH patients was subopti-mal, basing only on the diagnostic code: for psoriasis, patientswere identified also according to the prescribed treatment, butthis was not the case for AIH patients. Psoriasis is treated withanti-TNF agents, which are known to trigger AIH: therefore,in this case, AIH is linked to the drug and not to psoriasis[42, 43]. In the aforementioned Danish study, the threepatients with psoriasis were not treated by anti-TNF. Aseries of 158 AIH patients from Greece [44] found aprevalence of psoriasis of 2.5%, slightly higher than theexpected prevalence in Southern Europe. Further studiesare needed before an association between psoriasis andAIH can be confirmed, which is possible according tothese data.In cohorts of AIH patients, the most frequent skin disordersreported in association with AIH are vitiligo, psoriasis, and

alopecia areata. Skin diseases are probably underdiagnosedand underreported in AIH: as an example, a large cohort fromJapan [29] including 866 patients did not report any case ofcoexisting skin disease. A cohort from the UK including 164AIH adult patients reported concomitance of vitiligo, alopeciaareata, Bvasculitis,^ and erythema nodosum, but the exactnumber of patients affected is not reported [45].A prospective case collection from a large Indian gastroen-terology center identified 41 AIH cases, of which 2 (5%) hadconcomitant vitiligo, a tenfold higher vitiligo prevalence thanin the general population. Of note, not all patients underwentliver biopsy and anti-LC-1 antibodies were not assessed,therefore the cohort was not accurately characterized.Another retrospective series from India including 79 AIH pa-tients, reported one case of vitiligo and three cases of erythemanodosum [46]. A series of AIH patients from a single Italiancenter found that 13/327 (3.8%) patients had a concomitantskin disease including alopecia, vitiligo, and psoriasis, but theexact number of patients affected by each disease was notreported [27]. A series of 143 patients from Brazil is of par-ticular interest, since 82% of them had pediatric onset AIH:patients with concomitant extrahepatic autoimmune diseaseswere older than patients without such conditions (median age24 years in the group with extrahepatic manifestations,11 years in the group without, p = 0.02) [47]. Vitiligo wasreported in 1/115 type 1 AIH patient (1%) and in 1/28 type2 patients (4%), discoid lupus was found in 2/115 type 1patients, and in none of the 28 type 2 AIH patients.Scleroderma was reported in 1/115 type 1 AIH, and Weberpanniculitis in 1/28 type 2 AIH patients. Overall, extrahepaticautoimmune diseases were less frequent in this cohort in com-parison with adult cohorts, pointing out that such conditionscould be due to the longer life span in adults and can occurafter the diagnosis of AIH. This finding was confirmed by acohort from Argentina comparing children (n = 122) andadults (n = 84) with AIH: 13% of the pediatric cases hadconcomitant extra-hepatic autoimmune diseases, as comparedwith 39% of the adult cases (p = 0.0002). In this cohort, 5/122(4%) children and 3/84 (3.6%) had concomitant skin diseasesincluding psoriasis and vitiligo, but the exact number of pa-tients affected by either disease was not reported. Anotherpediatric, prospective study from the UK identified the onlycase of concomitant urticaria pigmentosa [22]. A very recentreport on concurrent extrahepatic autoimmunity in a large co-hort of 562 AIH patients from the UK [30] reported a frequen-cy of 8% for autoimmune skin diseases, with 6 cases each ofvitiligo and alopecia areata (1%), a frequency higher than inthe general population, where vitiligo affects 0.5 of the popu-lation [48] and alopecia areata 0.1-0.2% [49]. In this series,the precise dermatological diagnosis of the remaining 33 iden-tified cases was not reported, the only information availablebeing that patients with AIH type 2 were more frequentlyaffected by concurrent autoimmune skin diseases than patients

A hallmark paper by the Daniel Alagille group inParis reported for the first-time type 2 AIH [50], describ-ing a series of 65 cases of Bchronic active hepatitis^ pos-itive for anti-LKM-1 antibodies, 44% of them aged be-tween 2 and 14 years: 2/65 patients had a first-degreerelative affected by vitiligo. Vitiligo was also found in 6of the 65 described cases (10.5%), and no other skinconditions were reported. In the same paper, anti-LKM-1-positive patients were compared with 60 patients withBchronic active hepatitis^ positive for anti-SMA, none ofwhich had vitiligo. Taken together, these data suggest aparticularly high prevalence of vitiligo in type 2 AIH,much higher than the vitiligo prevalence in the generalpopulation of 1, and 0.25% under the age of 10 years(Fig. 3) [51].A lot of case reports of cutaneous diseases in AIHpatients have been published (Table 1), pointing out theclinician's awareness of the frequent coexistence of sev-eral autoimmune diseases in the same patient but notallowing any information about the frequency of the re-ported skin conditions in AIH patients. Erythemanodosum has been reported in five AIH patients, one adult[26], one child [52], and three adults from India [46]. Three case reports of coexisting Sweet's syn-drome have been published [54-56]. Interestingly, onecase of amicrobial pustulosis of the folds, a rare neutro-philic dermatosis often associated with lupus erythemato-sus, has been reported in association with AIH [57]. Ofnote, in another small dermatological series of five pa-tients affected by amicrobial pustulosis of the folds [34],one patient has positive anti-SMA and ANA, and raisedtransaminase levels, suggesting a possible concomitantAIH. However, the report focused on dermatological as-pects, and no more information about extra-cutaneous dis-ease is provided.Pyoderma gangrenosum (PG) is a severe neutrophilicdermatosis classically associated with IBD. Severalcases have been reported in association with AIH[58-63], both in children and in adults. The concomi-tant diseases are not systematically reported, so that itcannot be excluded that some patients were also affect-ed by IBD. In addition to the case reports, the preva-lence of AIH has been assessed in two large cohorts ofPG patients. The first series of 86 patients seen at theMayo Clinic between 1970 and 1983 found two caseswith concomitant AIH (2.3%) [64]; the second serieswhich included 103 PG patients seen at theMassachusetts General Hospital between 2000 and2007 found two cases of concomitant AIH (1.9%)[33]. These findings suggest an association betweenPG and AIH.In conclusion, the present literature review highlights aprobable association of vitiligo with AIH, particularly type2. Other possible associations of AIH are with alopecia areata,psoriasis, and pyoderma gangrenosum.Primary Biliary Cholangitis

PBC, formerly called primary biliary cirrhosis [65-72], is achronic autoimmune inflammation of the small and mediumintrahepatic bile ducts [73] with variable clinical course, main-ly affecting women with a female-to-male ratio of 10:1 [74]and a median age at diagnosis around 50 years [75]. It isconsidered a rare disease, but reported incidence and preva-lence are rising, probably due to various reasons [76]. Thedisease is virtually absent in children. The two more prevalentsymptoms, affecting up to 80% of patients, are pruritusand fatigue [77]. The serological hallmarks of PBC areanti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA), which are foundin 90-95% of the cases and are disease specific [75].ANA are detected by indirect immunofluorescence (IIF)in about half of the patients [77]. Two IIF patterns aredisease specific: the rim-like and the multiple nucleardot (MND) patterns. They are generated by autoantibodyreaction mainly with glycoprotein 210 or nucleoporin 62and with Sp100 or the promyelocytic leukemia antigen,respectively [78]. The presence of these PBC-specificANA is associated with a more severe disease course[79, 80]. The diagnosis is based on the presence of at leasttwo of the following criteria: elevation of alkaline phos-phatase of hepatic origin for at least 6 months, positiveAMA and/or PBC-specific ANA, and diagnostic or com-patible liver histology [81-83]. First-line treatment isursodeoxycholic acid, 13/15 mg/kg lifelong. Patients nor-malizing or nearly normalizing the biochemical cholesta-sis in absence of advanced disease at diagnosis, have anormal life expectancy [84]. Second-line treatments areobeticholic acid or fibrates.PBC is associated with concomitant extrahepatic diseases,including skin diseases, in up to 60% of the cases [27, 85]. Themost frequent extrahepatic autoimmune diseases are Sjogren'ssyndrome and Hashimoto thyroiditis, their presence is notassociated to a different clinical course, except for a decreasedtendency to liver fibrosis in association with scleroderma [86].Skin manifestations in PBC have been recognized sincelong time, particularly pruritus, which is a typical presentingsymptom in PBC [81]. Butterfly sign has been reported as atypical skin finding in PBC, secondary to pruritus: it is char-acterized by normal skin on the upper back surrounded byhyperpigmentation secondary to scratching [87].Hyperlipidemia is frequent in PBC patients but not associatedwith an increased cardio-vascular risk [88]. As a consequence,xanthomata are a common finding in PBC. In this context, it isworthy to remember that the first name used for PBC wasBxantomathous biliary cirrhosis^ [89, 90]. Xanthelasma(Fig. 4) and xanthoma striatum palmare are additionalxanthomatoses reported in association with PBC [91-94].Increased cutaneous pigmentation is a frequent clinical find-ing in PBC patients [95, 96].

The most frequently reported skin disease associatedwith PBC is lichen planus [97] (Fig. 5; Table 2).However, this high incidence rate is probably partiallyinduced by D-penicillamine exposure [98, 99], a drug thathas been used in the past to treat PBC [100] for its slightlyimmunosuppressive action. This drug is still used for Wilson'sdisease and is known to induce skin rash in up to 5% of thepatients, including lichen planus. The association of lichenplanus with chronic hepatitis C is well established, and thisrecognition has contributed to the recognition of the associa-tion between liver disease and lichen planus [101]. On thisbackground, one series of 54 patients with oral lichen planusfound only one case of PBC [102], and another large series of577 patients with lichen planus only found one PBC case[102, 103]. Subsequently, a subanalysis of the 50 patients withliver disease included in the latter series did not report anyPBC case, but only biopsy-proven PBC was considered, andonly 12 out of the 50 patients underwent liver biopsy. A veryinteresting study looked for anti-MND autoantibodies in seraof a large cohort of patients with autoimmune diseases withpredominantly cutaneous features. Nine-thousand-eighty-ninesera from 6240 patients were analyzed, 15 patients were pos-itive for anti-MND autoantibodies, 2 of which affected bylichen planus and 1 by telogen effluvium [104]. None of thepositive patients had PBC (normal liver biochemistry).Although 2/15 may appear a high frequency, it should be keptin mind that anti-MND autoantibodies are found in a smallproportion of patients with autoimmune diseases other thanPBC [78], as the population included in the study was.Taken together, all these data confirm that lichen planus isnot significantly associated with PBC.AMA are part of the proposed diagnostic criteria foramicrobial pustulosis of the folds, a rare neutrophilic derma-tosis occurring almost only in young female patients in asso-ciation with various autoimmune diseases [57]. However, on-ly two cases have been reported so far, both without a reported

diagnosis of PBC but only AMA positivity [34]. Of note,AMA positivity is reported in the only published case ofamicrobial pustulosis of the folds associated with AIH [57].More joint efforts between hepatologists and dermatologistsare needed to establish a link between the two conditions.A questionnaire-based study from France in a cohort of 222PBC patients and 509 controls found a significantly highernumber of PBC patients affected by vitiligo (4/205 versus0/509, p = 0.0067) than controls, but no difference in thenumber of patients affected by psoriasis [105]. A case-control study from the UK, again questionnaire-based, foundthat a personal history of psoriasis was associated with anincreased risk of PBC (Fig. 6) [106], confirming a previousstudy by the same group, which had found a significantlyhigher prevalence of psoriasis in PBC patients than controls(13 versus 3%, odds ratio 4.6). A large questionnaire-basedstudy from the USA [107] did not report psoriasis among riskfactors for PBC. Therefore, the association of PBC with pso-riasis is still to be confirmed.Vitiligo was found in 1.9% of 205 PBC patients in one se-ries [105] and in 9% in a small series of 22 PBC patients [108];however, in a large Italian series of 281 PBC patients, no caseof concomitant skin diseases was found. These conflictingdata on associated dermatological conditions need furtherevaluation [97].In conclusion, the available literature does not allow todraw reliable conclusions on any skin disease associated withPBC, although some data suggest a possible association withvitiligo, psoriasis, and the very rare amicrobial pustulosis ofthe folds.Primary Sclerosing CholangitisPSC is a rare chronic cholestatic liver disease, with progres-sive inflammation and fibrosis of the medium and large,Clinic Rev Allerg ImmunolAutoimmune bullous diseasesPemphigus superficialis11985 USAGibson et al. It affects allages, although it is still not clear if the pediatric conditionreferred to as autoimmune sclerosing cholangitis is part ofthe same disease spectrum [22]. In adults, the incidence rangesfrom 0 to 1.3 per 100,000 inhabitants per year, and the prev-alence ranges from 0 to 16.2 per 100,000 inhabitants [109].There is a strong association with IBD, with up to 80% of PSCpatients being affected by IBD as well [109]. Sixty percent ofthe patients are male, median age at diagnosis is 41 years[110]. The diagnosis is based on bile duct strictures detectedby endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography ormagnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography, associatedwith a biochemical cholestatic pattern, after having ruledout secondary forms. Approximately 5-10% of patientshave small bile duct PSC, which has a more benigncourse [109]. Ursodeoxycholic acid is frequently adminis-tered to PSC patients, although guidelines are conflicting,due to lack of solid evidence of a survival benefit. PSC isassociated, beside IBD, with extrahepatic autoimmune dis-eases, but literature is scanty on this topic, and provides onlycase reports, many of them reporting skin diseases in patientsaffected by both PSC and IBD. In IBD, the association withpyoderma gangrenosum, erythema nodosum, and neutrophilicdermatoses is well established, so that it is difficult to establishan association between PSC and cutaneous diseases, since thevast majority of PSC patients have concomitant IBD.Cutaneous diseases associated with IBD are discussed in adifferent article of this issue of Clinical reviews in allergyand immunology. Table 3 summarizes the reported cases ofPSC patients having cutaneous diseases, excluding patientswith concomitant IBD and either pyoderma gangrenosum,erythema nodosum, or neutrophilic dermatoses. Of particularinterest is one case report from Japan of a young womandiagnosed with dermatitis herpetiformis at the age of18 years, who later developed PSC and IBD and died atage 40 because of end-stage liver disease. She did nothave celiac disease. In the case of a 1.5-year-old childwith cutaneous vasculitis and sclerosing cholangitis in

the context of Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, a genetic dis-ease leading to immunodeficiency and malignancies, bileduct disease was more probably due to the immunodefi-ciency condition rather than to PSC [111].Besides case reports, only one series of PSC patientsinvestigating concomitant non-IBD immunological dis-eases is available. In this prospective cohort of 195 PSCpatients from a German tertiary center, psoriasis wasfound in 3.6% of patients and represented a risk factorfor reduced survival [112]. This percentage is similar tothe estimated psoriasis prevalence in this country [113],making an association of PSC with psoriasis unlikely.Variant SyndromesA subgroup of patients with AIH present with clinical, sero-logical, histological, and radiological features of either PBC orPSC. The term var-iant syndrome has been proposed for this purpose [114].Diagnostic criteria for AIH/PBC variant syndrome have beenpublished [115]. In pediatrics, about half of the children diag-nosed with AIH present with abnormal cholangiogram: thiscondition has been named autoimmune sclerosing cholangitis[22]. Evidence-based treatment for variant syndromes is notavailable, and patients are empirically treated for bothdiseases.One single cohort of patients affected by AIH/PBC variantsyndrome has investigated concomitant extrahepatic autoim-munity [116]. A total of 71 adult patients were included fromItaly, Turkey, France, and Sweden: two of them (3%) hadconcomitant vitiligo and three (4.2%) had concomitant psori-asis. The frequency of vitiligo is higher than in the generalpopulation, but it is difficult to draw any conclusion for pso-riasis, since its frequency is different according to differentgeographical areas, being as high as 8.5% in Norway and2.9% in Italy [117], following a North-South gradient.

A few case reports on concomitant autoimmune skin dis-eases in PSC/AIH patients are available, including one case ofcutaneous amyloidosis [118] and two cases of vitiligo [119,120] (Table 4). One case of psoriasis is also reported in apatient with concomitant PBC and PSC, an extremely rareoverlapping condition [121]. Based on these few data, a pos-sible association of vitiligo and variant syndrome exists: this isnot surprising, since AIH is part of the variant syndrome.GeneticsOne of the major disappointments in autoimmunity has been therelative failure to identify genetic Bsmoking guns.^ Nonetheless,it is clear that various autoimmune diseases tend to cluster. Forexample, lupus clearly clusters with scleroderma, Sjogren's syn-drome, and rheumatoid arthritis. Thus, within a family and withinan affected individual, these diseases are more likely to occur.Similarly, the endocrine autoimmune syndromes cluster with vit-iligo and often celiac sprue. *PSC and IBDClinic Rev Allerg ImmunolTo extend this analogy, we suggest that, as deep sequencingexpands, particularly in family studies, there will be clearergenetic markers that may identify associated autoimmunemanifestations of a given disease, likely to give statisticalcredence to some of the dermal associations described herein.This is an area beyond the scope of this paper and this specialissue, but we will cite a number of recent reviews and cuttingedge experimental papers that focus on this subject [233-239].ConclusionsThis systematic literature review on skin diseases associated withautoimmune liver diseases highlights some associations. AIH isprobably significantly associated with vitiligo, in particular withthe rare pediatric type 2 AIH. Other less strong associations arewith alopecia areata, psoriasis, and pyoderma gangrenosum.PBC is not significantly associated with lichen planus. Possibleassociations are with vitiligo, psoriasis, and the very rareamicrobial pustulosis of the folds. For PSC, it is difficult to iden-tify associations with skin diseases, since 80% of the patientshave concomitant IBD, which is associated with neutrophilicdermatoses and erythema nodosum. Based on only one study,there is a possible association with psoriasis. Variant syndromes,which are not a different disease as the predominant autoimmuneliver disease, are also associated with vitiligo. Overall, the quan-tity and quality of the data are scanty, and in the future, epidemi-ological studies should be more accurate in reporting skin man-ifestations associated with autoimmune liver diseases.Compliance with Ethical StandardsConflicts of InterestThe authors declare that they have no con-flict of interest.Ethical ApprovalNot applicable.
